payload.text: Egg and dietary-cholesterol consumption meaningfully raises cardiovascular-disease and mortality risk for most people.
payload.title: Eggs meaningfully raise CVD and mortality risk

payload.text: A meta-analysis of 28 randomized controlled trials found egg consumption raised total cholesterol by about 5.6 mg/dL, LDL-C by about 5.55 mg/dL, and HDL-C by about 2.13 mg/dL relative to low-egg control diets.
payload.title: RCT meta-analysis: egg effect on TC/LDL/HDL (s04)

payload.text: Egg consumption raises LDL cholesterol only modestly in randomized-controlled-trial evidence.
payload.title: Modest LDL rise from eggs (RCT evidence)

payload.text: Egg consumption raises HDL cholesterol alongside LDL, leaving cholesterol ratios (LDL:HDL, TC:HDL) roughly unchanged.
payload.title: Eggs raise HDL too - ratios roughly unchanged

payload.text: A 2025 randomized cross-over trial (61 adults, three 5-week isocaloric diets) found that a high-cholesterol diet including 2 eggs/day did not raise LDL-C relative to control, and slightly lowered it.
payload.title: 2025 crossover RCT: eggs did not raise LDL-C (s05)

payload.text: Across the diets in the same 2025 randomized cross-over trial, dietary saturated-fat intake was significantly related to LDL-C, but dietary-cholesterol intake was not.
payload.title: 2025 crossover RCT: sat fat, not cholesterol, tracked LDL-C (s05)

payload.text: Saturated fat, not the cholesterol in eggs, is the main driver of LDL-cholesterol elevation.
payload.title: Saturated fat, not dietary cholesterol, drives LDL (2025 crux)

payload.text: Dietary cholesterol meaningfully raises serum LDL cholesterol for most people.
payload.title: Dietary cholesterol meaningfully raises LDL

payload.text: A review argues the evidence linking dietary cholesterol to cardiovascular disease is weak, because dietary cholesterol has a limited and variable effect on serum cholesterol.
payload.title: Review: dietary-cholesterol-CVD evidence is weak (s13)

payload.text: Dietary cholesterol has a limited and variable effect on serum cholesterol for most people.
payload.title: Dietary cholesterol's effect on serum cholesterol is limited/variable

payload.text: A randomized controlled trial reported that adding eggs to the diet did not significantly raise blood cholesterol.
payload.title: RCT: adding eggs did not significantly raise blood cholesterol (s06)

payload.text: A review notes individuals differ in how strongly dietary cholesterol raises serum cholesterol ('hyper-responders' vs 'hypo-responders'), complicating population-wide dietary advice.
payload.title: Hyper- vs hypo-responder variation (s13)

payload.text: Individual variation in cholesterol response ('hyper-responder' vs 'hypo-responder') means a single population-wide dietary-cholesterol recommendation does not fit everyone.
payload.title: Hyper-/hypo-responder variation complicates universal advice

payload.text: A 2025 randomized cross-over trial measuring egg and saturated-fat effects on LDL-C is a small, short-term trial on surrogate lipid endpoints, not on cardiovascular events.
payload.title: 2025 crossover RCT measures surrogate lipid endpoints, not CV events (s05)

payload.text: Blood-lipid measurements such as LDL, HDL, and total cholesterol in RCTs are surrogate markers, not direct measurements of cardiovascular events or mortality.
payload.title: Lipid RCT endpoints are surrogates, not hard CV outcomes

payload.text: Three large US prospective cohorts plus an updated dose-response meta-analysis found that moderate egg consumption, up to about one egg per day, was not associated with cardiovascular-disease risk in the general population.
payload.title: US cohorts + meta: no CVD link up to ~1 egg/day

payload.text: A recent review of the egg-consumption literature concludes that for the general population, egg consumption is not clearly associated with cardiovascular-disease risk.
payload.title: Review: no clear general-population CVD link

payload.text: The same review notes the overall evidence base on egg consumption and cardiovascular disease is mixed and dominated by observational studies of modest effect size.
payload.title: Review: evidence base mixed, mostly observational

payload.text: A dose-response meta-analysis pooling about 55 studies (~2.7 million individuals) found each additional egg per day associated with about 7% higher all-cause mortality and about 13% higher cancer mortality.
payload.title: Dose-response meta: +7%/day all-cause mortality

payload.text: In the same dose-response meta-analysis, egg consumption was not significantly associated with cardiovascular-disease mortality specifically.
payload.title: Same meta-analysis: no CVD-mortality signal

payload.text: An updated meta-analysis of about 41 prospective cohort studies found higher dietary-cholesterol and egg consumption associated with increased overall and cardiovascular-disease mortality.
payload.title: 41-cohort meta: higher overall + CVD mortality

payload.text: In the same updated meta-analysis, the egg-cardiovascular-disease association was geographically heterogeneous: a significant positive association in US cohorts, a marginal association in European cohorts, and no association in Asian cohorts.
payload.title: Geographic heterogeneity: US positive, Asia null

payload.text: In the same updated meta-analysis, each 100 mg/day increment in dietary cholesterol was associated with higher all-cause and cancer mortality but not clearly with cardiovascular-disease mortality.
payload.title: Cholesterol dose-response: all-cause up, CVD unclear

payload.text: A large US population-based cohort study found higher egg and dietary-cholesterol intake associated with higher all-cause and cardiovascular-disease mortality, with the association driven substantially by cholesterol content rather than eggs per se.
payload.title: US cohort: mortality link driven by cholesterol content

payload.text: In the same cohort study, statistically substituting egg whites or other protein sources for whole eggs was associated with lower mortality.
payload.title: Egg-white substitution associated with lower mortality

payload.text: A longitudinal cohort study found that egg consumption's association with cardiovascular disease is modified by type-2-diabetes status: elevated cardiovascular-disease risk appears among people with diabetes at higher egg intake, but not clearly in the general population.
payload.title: T2D modifies egg-CVD association

payload.text: The same study characterizes the diabetes-subgroup interaction as one of the more consistent signals in the egg-consumption literature.
payload.title: Diabetes-subgroup signal called relatively consistent

payload.text: A systematic review and meta-analysis found no overall egg-cardiovascular-disease association in the general population but an increased cardiovascular-disease risk among people with diabetes eating more eggs.
payload.title: Meta-analysis: null in general population, positive in diabetes

payload.text: In the same review, pooled estimates indicate that people with type-2 diabetes eating more than about seven eggs per week have substantially more cardiovascular-disease events, on the order of 69% more, than those eating few eggs.
payload.title: T2D + >7 eggs/week: ~69% more CVD events

payload.text: For the general population, egg consumption up to about one egg per day is not associated with meaningfully increased cardiovascular-disease risk.
payload.title: General population: no meaningful CVD-risk increase up to ~1/day

payload.text: The mortality association found for egg or dietary-cholesterol intake in several cohort meta-analyses is driven mainly by all-cause and cancer mortality, not by cardiovascular-disease-specific mortality.
payload.title: Mortality signal is mainly all-cause/cancer, not CVD-specific

payload.text: Egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption is associated with increased cardiovascular-disease-specific mortality in some updated cohort meta-analyses, not only all-cause mortality.
payload.title: Some meta-analyses find a CVD-specific mortality signal

payload.text: The small increase in all-cause mortality associated with higher egg intake reflects a real causal effect of egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption.
payload.title: Mortality signal is a real causal effect of eggs

payload.text: The small increase in all-cause mortality associated with higher egg intake reflects residual confounding, for example by dietary pattern or other lifestyle factors correlated with egg consumption, rather than a causal effect of eggs.
payload.title: Mortality signal reflects residual confounding, not eggs

payload.text: The geographic heterogeneity of the egg-cardiovascular/mortality association (positive in US cohorts, marginal in European cohorts, null in Asian cohorts) is evidence that the association reflects confounding by dietary pattern or lifestyle rather than a direct causal effect of eggs.
payload.title: Geographic heterogeneity points to confounding

payload.text: The geographic heterogeneity in the egg-cardiovascular/mortality association may instead reflect real differences in egg-consumption dose, food preparation, or population susceptibility across regions, rather than purely confounding.
payload.title: Heterogeneity may reflect real dose/susceptibility differences

payload.text: Among people with type-2 diabetes, higher egg intake, more than about seven eggs per week, is associated with meaningfully elevated cardiovascular-disease risk, even though this pattern is not clearly present in the general population.
payload.title: Diabetes subgroup: elevated CVD risk at higher egg intake

payload.text: The elevated cardiovascular-disease risk seen with higher egg intake in the type-2-diabetes subgroup reflects a real causal effect of eggs or dietary cholesterol on cardiovascular risk in that population.
payload.title: Diabetes-subgroup risk is a real causal effect of eggs

payload.text: The elevated cardiovascular-disease risk seen with higher egg intake in the type-2-diabetes subgroup reflects confounding by other diabetes-related dietary or lifestyle factors rather than a causal effect of eggs.
payload.title: Diabetes-subgroup risk reflects confounding, not eggs

payload.text: Prospective observational evidence on egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption and chronic-disease outcomes is mixed, with increased, decreased, and null associations all reported across studies.
payload.title: Egg-disease evidence base: mixed observational findings (s16)

payload.text: No long-term randomized controlled trials of egg consumption on hard clinical endpoints -- cardiovascular events or all-cause/cardiovascular mortality -- exist.
payload.title: No long-term RCTs on hard endpoints exist (s16)

payload.text: Prospective cohort estimates of the egg-consumption/chronic-disease association are limited by residual confounding -- healthy-user bias, socioeconomic factors, and broader dietary-pattern factors -- that observational statistical adjustment cannot fully remove.
payload.title: Residual confounding limits cohort estimates (s16)

payload.text: A long-term double-blind randomized controlled trial of egg consumption on cardiovascular events is impractical to conduct, so the field must rely on imperfect observational evidence instead.
payload.title: Long-term hard-endpoint RCT on eggs is impractical (s16)

payload.text: Because egg-consumption studies are predominantly observational, an association between egg or dietary-cholesterol intake and a health outcome does not by itself establish that egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption causes that outcome.
payload.title: Observational association does not establish causation

payload.text: Residual confounding that observational statistical adjustment cannot fully remove means cohort-observed associations between egg consumption and cardiovascular-disease or mortality outcomes may partly or wholly reflect confounding rather than a causal effect of eggs.
payload.title: Cohort associations may reflect unremovable residual confounding

payload.text: Randomized-controlled-trial evidence that eggs modestly affect LDL cholesterol, a surrogate marker, does not by itself establish eggs' effect on cardiovascular events or mortality, the clinical endpoints the question is actually about.
payload.title: Surrogate-endpoint RCT evidence does not settle clinical-endpoint effects

payload.text: Whether egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption causally raises cardiovascular-disease and mortality risk for most people remains genuinely unresolved by the current evidence base.
payload.title: The causal question remains genuinely unresolved

payload.text: The 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans removed the prior recommendation to limit dietary cholesterol to under 300 mg/day.
payload.title: 2015 guidelines drop the 300mg cholesterol cap

payload.text: The Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee for the 2015-2020 guidelines stated that cholesterol is 'not a nutrient of concern for overconsumption,' citing a lack of clear evidence for the specific 300 mg/day cap.
payload.title: Committee: cholesterol 'not a nutrient of concern'

payload.text: The 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines nonetheless advised keeping dietary cholesterol intake as low as practical, even after removing the numeric cap.
payload.title: Guidelines retained 'as low as practical' cholesterol advice

payload.text: Nutrition commentary on the 2015 Dietary Guidelines noted that removing the numeric cholesterol cap while retaining softer cholesterol advice reflected genuine scientific uncertainty about a specific numeric limit.
payload.title: Commentary: cap removal reflects genuine uncertainty

payload.text: The original 300 mg/day dietary-cholesterol cap originated from mid-20th-century guidance (AHA 1968; U.S. Dietary Goals 1977) rather than from a trial establishing that specific threshold.
payload.title: 300mg cap's mid-20th-century origin, not a trial

payload.text: A US children's nutrition-survey study found egg consumption associated with greater daily intake of protein, lutein and zeaxanthin, choline, alpha-linolenic acid, and DHA, without displacing other nutrients from the diet.
payload.title: Egg consumption linked to higher nutrient intake (children's survey)

payload.text: Eggs are among the few concentrated dietary sources of choline, a nutrient many people under-consume.
payload.title: Eggs as a concentrated choline source

payload.text: A consumer-health source states eggs are a low-cost source of high-quality complete protein (about 6 g per large egg) with high satiety, useful for weight management.
payload.title: Consumer source: eggs' protein/satiety for weight management (blog)

payload.text: A consumer-health source states egg yolks contain lutein and zeaxanthin, antioxidants associated with reduced risk of cataracts and macular degeneration.
payload.title: Consumer source: lutein/zeaxanthin and eye-disease risk (blog)

payload.text: Eggs are a nutrient-dense food, supplying complete protein and concentrated amounts of several under-consumed nutrients including choline, lutein, and zeaxanthin.
payload.title: Eggs are nutrient-dense

payload.text: Eggs' high protein content and satiety help support effective weight management.
payload.title: Eggs support weight management via protein/satiety

payload.text: Lutein and zeaxanthin from egg yolks are associated with reduced risk of cataracts and age-related macular degeneration.
payload.title: Lutein/zeaxanthin and reduced eye-disease risk

payload.text: The 2015-2020 removal of the 300 mg/day dietary-cholesterol cap reflected an evidence-based reassessment that dietary cholesterol is not a population-level nutrient of concern.
payload.title: Cap removal = evidence-based reassessment

payload.text: The 2015-2020 guidelines' retained advice to keep dietary cholesterol intake 'as low as practical' despite removing the numeric cap shows the science on dietary cholesterol was not fully settled, not a clean exoneration.
payload.title: Cap removal left genuine scientific caution, not exoneration

payload.text: For most people, eating eggs in moderation is a reasonable dietary choice.
payload.title: Eating eggs is reasonable for most people

payload.text: A precautionary approach to egg and dietary-cholesterol intake is warranted for most people.
payload.title: Precaution is warranted for most people

payload.text: Egg and dietary-cholesterol consumption does not meaningfully raise cardiovascular-disease risk for most people.
payload.title: Eggs do not meaningfully raise CVD risk

payload.text: Eggs provide real nutritional benefits -- complete protein, choline, lutein and zeaxanthin, and satiety that supports weight management.
payload.title: Eggs provide real nutritional benefits

payload.text: The trial evidence that could settle egg causation on hard clinical endpoints is absent or measures only surrogate markers.
payload.title: No trial evidence on real clinical endpoints

payload.text: The available observational and subgroup evidence cannot establish whether eggs causally affect health outcomes for most people.
payload.title: Observational evidence can't pin down causation

payload.text: A food with real nutritional benefits and only modest, unproven risks is a reasonable thing to eat.
payload.title: Value: benefits justify a modest unproven risk

payload.text: When a plausible health harm is genuinely unresolved, limiting exposure is prudent, especially for higher-risk groups.
payload.title: Value: limit exposure under unresolved harm

payload.reason: Consumer-health/blog source (s15) restating its own dependent claim n058 (eggs' protein/satiety support weight management); a blog is not the source of these nutritional facts and the claim is already carried by n058. n058 remains as a claim.

Consumer-health/blog source (s15) restating its own dependent claim n059 (yolk lutein/zeaxanthin and eye-disease risk); a blog is not the source of these facts and the claim is already carried by n059. n059 remains as a claim.
